text: I am very pleased today to welcome Jad Abam Rad, the founding host of WNYC's Peabody Award
winning radio show and podcast series radio lab.
Radio lab which has been running since 2002 is a show about curiosity, where sound
illuminates ideas and the boundaries blur between science, philosophy and human experience.
Jad is the son of a scientist and a doctor and grew up in Tennessee before studying creative
writing and music composition at Oberlin.
Following graduation, he wrote music for films and reported and produced documentaries for
a variety of local and national public radio programs.
He has been working on radio lab for almost 15 years.
Let's give a warm round of applause for Jad.
A lot of radio lab fans at Google.
It seems so I want to start off by talking about the announcement that you made on Facebook
last year saying that for the first time since its inception you would be taking a bit of
the hiatus from Radio Lab.
In my mind you were on a beach somewhere, composing music but somehow imagine that's probably
not how your sabbatical went.
I took four months off and I actually am just a week back.
What did I do?
How did you spend your time?
I had a lot of bickroom yoga.
No, I did not at all.
What did I do?
I don't know, it's funny.
The reason I took the break was because I, it's funny radio lab began kind of as a stumble into
the dark and suddenly 15 years went by.
It was a chance to just take a beat and take a breath and reintroduce myself to my kids.
I just take them to school and pick them up from school.
Really that's kind of what I did.
I sat in my studio and broke them a lot and then I took my kids to school and I picked them up.
It was just a chance to not be on the constant deadline treadmill for a while.
It was great.
I come back to the show now.
So many ideas and just ready to do another 15 years.
It was just a moment I needed at that point.
I did nothing.
It was a short answer.
I really like defiantly did nothing.
That's actually very reassuring because I think people who are as productive and prolific as
you would probably have a lot of difficulties slowing down and doing nothing for a few months.
They were hard.
The first month, my brain is very used to output.
You start to measure your value as a human by how much stuff did you put?
How many minutes did you put out there to be consumed?
When your brain is still doing that but there are no minutes going out, it can be hard.
I had a couple of weeks that were difficult.
But then I got to a place where I was before radio lab, which was as a well-educated bomb.
That was a nice place to be for a while.
You said a few moments ago that the beginning of radio lab was a stumble.
I'd like to just take it back and ask you some very basic questions about the genesis of the show
and how it started.
Can you talk about how it came to be back in the early 2000s?
Yeah, 2002, early, 2002, I guess would be the beginning.
It's weird to talk about because radio lab as a thing with a name existed from that point,
but it's morphed and changed into so many things that I could easily say the beginning was 2004 or 2006.
But 2002, the guy who actually brought me here, he was staying right back there, Michael Assessor,
and he and I had a crazy idea to make a space on the Anne frequency in New York called radio lab.
The idea was that it would just be a laboratory, it would be just an experimental space.
At that point, there was the meme in circulation through our industry, which was let's have a DJ of documentary space
where there are all these documentaries that exist in the world of public radio that have been produced in Australia.
There's BBC and Canada and people work really, really hard on these things and then they just sit on the shelf and gather dust.
So let's create a place on the radio where we could take some of that old content and mix it up and recombine it and remix it.
I was that guy, that was my mandate, which was to play old documentaries, but to also experiment with new forms.
And I did that for two years on the AM frequency and nobody was listening.
And actually I think that, I mean that quite literally, because it turns out there's this thing I would later learn about the properties of AM radio waves,
which is that they're very long waves and so they travel a great distances.
And here in New York, they would bounce off the Hudson River and land in Canada and screw up their radio stations.
And so the FCC mandated that we cut the power on our signal at exactly the moment when my show came on.
So you literally could not hear radio lab.
Until when I say no one was listening because I couldn't. It wasn't because I was bad, which was also the case.
And so I basically broadcasted to no one for two years, which was I think actually really good.
It was like a very needed state of neglect that I need to go through.
Just to sort of figure out basic things, because I mean Michael to his credit took a risk on me when I did not warrant that risk.
I mean I was super green, I didn't know what I was doing.
So for two years I just kind of tried, how can I be like I reglaced but not sound like him.
Like that was the first year or so.
And also I was coming from music school and I had all these weird avant-garde notions of how to
you sound and how much of that stuff was appropriate. And when is the threshold when it just becomes really annoying.
And so like figuring out those kind of balance issues, what kind of stories are interesting to me.
I had to figure that out. How far do you put your mouth in front of the mic?
You know that turns out to be a really hard thing to figure out.
Because if you're really close, first is kind of like this, it's a huge difference in sound.
So I have to figure out where is my mic position? All those kind of talked about in questions.
And then somewhere I'm going to say a date, probably wrong 2003, 2004.
I met Robert in a total happenstance cycle sort of way.
One of the, I'm giving you the long story. Is that okay?
That's great. I like it.
You can do this if it gets too long.
I'll interrupt you.
Okay. And uh,
one of the ways that I had made radio lab work for the station, because it was a hard show to justify.
Because at that point there wasn't really anything like it.
Most of radio was just like turn on the mic and talk.
And I was like doing thousands of edits a second and like that was just not the form that made sense to anyone.
But one of the ways I justified it to WNYC was I would also do a bunch of promos for them to just station wide.
And so I made myself useful to the station beyond just the show.
And one of the promos I had to make was to go and interview a guy named Robert Crowwich about why WNYC
should be independent from the city at that time.
And it was just one of those weird moments.
So now I know it's quite familiar with Robert, because I have it with him every day.
But like you go in and I hand him a thing to read.
He's like I'm not going to read this.
And he like rips it up and throws it over his head.
He didn't really, but in my memory that's what he did.
And uh, and he then turns around and writes some crazy,
manic, brilliant riff about alien tycoons and oil-rich things.
And it was one moment where he's making fun of Giuliani in there.
And he just read that and I was like wow, who is this guy?
And so we actually just started to talk in that moment and turn that we had all these things in common.
We both worked at NPR for a beat.
We both had worked at WBAR for a little while.
We both had went to Oberlin and it just felt like we were in some weird parallel track.
And so we started having breakfast.
And so as I was just starting to form radio lab,
I was having breakfast with Robert every month at this diner and 66th in Broadway.
And at some point maybe a year in he listened to it.
And he was like, oh, it's kind of interesting.
And so he decided he wanted to sort of play.
And so we would get together like six in the morning,
which is the only time that we're studio time.
And do these little experiments that I would sort of wedge into the show.
And those experiments went from like five minutes to seven minutes to 20 minutes.
And thankfully all of this stuff has been lost.
None of it has been said.
I tried to look some of it up when I had a very hard time.
It's like it's literally been erased from the internet and I'm so thankful for that.
We have old computers and hard drives that have them stored on them.
You know, I did find an old CD of one of the things we made.
But it was even later sort of stage Robert Jad kind of early years.
There was a lot I mean we did one thing I remember it was like a 25 minute.
It just obscenely gratuitous thing about birds.
And it was like taking the sounds of crows and pitching their call to make like a box fugue.
I can't even remember why we did that.
But it was like literally if you turn on the radio that day,
which you wouldn't have been able to because you couldn't hear the show,
you would have heard a crow coughing as a box fugue.
And like we did stuff like that that like I'm sort of proud of and embarrassed by an equal measure.
And it took us we stumbled our way through it.
And then around 2005 or so the station decided to move us to the FM.
And that was I think sort of the formal kind of coming out party for radio lab,
which unfortunately happened because we did it when Terry Gross was on vacation.
And so they put us for five days in a row in her spot.
And which was turns out people really liked Terry Gross.
So it was not a particularly good introduction.
But that was the beginning of 2006.
And then from then to now it's just been sort of slightly iterating, changing our focus.
But the core team being intact, you know.
Yeah that was a must have been a pretty big hold of the climb yourself out of having the Terry Gross army
hating you from off the bat.
I mean I'm curious since the beginning, since that period about 10 years ago,
where do you start when you're looking for a story to the stories come to you?
Do people some men or it's people you know or are you just sort of doodling and you know playing around on the internet?
And then you kind of spit ball with each other?
It's all of the really.
I mean all different ways.
They never come in any, it's hard to predict, you know.
I mean over the years the amount of time we spend looking has just gotten more and more and more.
It used to be we would take half an idea and then work really hard to figure out how to make it into a full thing.
But as you do more of those, you begin to sort of cross off the lifts, stuff you've already done.
These days I'd say we spend two thirds of the process just looking.
And so sometimes it's you're just reading a ton of things and looking for like those
story-shaped objects that have a big idea kind of inside of them.
A lot of these days a lot of it is talking to people, having breakfast with people.
Because like a lot of the ideas now come through the internet, you know, and everybody has the same ideas because everyone's going to the same research.
But so it's a lot of it is talking to people, a lot of it is
you build up a community of people who you just really are interested in know what they're interested in
and you're just constantly asking them, so like what's the last thing you read that really made you kind of
cock your head sideways or whatever it is. So a lot of it is conversations.
We have meetings every Friday where a lot of reporters will come from outside the staff, pitch a stuff.
So it's everything all at once. And you know, the stuff that we do often comes in half baked and takes a year,
sometimes two years to mature. So like you'll have like a half idea that you'll just have
stuck in your head or on the shelf for six months.
Then it starts to feel like a thing that you could commit to. But then from that point to when it hits the air,
it could be like 18 months, you know.
Yeah, that was going to be my next question. It's how long has it taken episode to complete?
It varies anywhere from six months to a year, I would say, on average. But there have been stories
that's taken two years. I mean we did a story recently about a hunter and Utah, I believe,
who paid 300 grand to go and kill an endangered black rhinoceros in the Mibia.
And the money was then going to be used for conservation. So it's this real like weird paradox where
like you're killing an endangered animal to save other animals. And we wanted to be there through
the whole process. And it took me a year to get the permits from fish and wildlife to go there.
And we basically followed it all the way through to the kill to just really sort of get the full
portrait of this is this modern conservation in a way.
So you start from a place where you have this specific story about about this black rhinoceros on the
center and then you link it to a kind of grander idea and then you fill another segments related to that
grander idea or is it kind of the other way around?
It's so no that's right the way you said the first time. I mean we used to do thematic shows.
So if you hear us on the radio what you will hear because we are, this is probably bears explaining
we are two things at once. We're a podcast primarily at this point and then we're a radio show
that's a weekly show. So if you're in your car and you hear us what you're going to hear is an
hour-long show which is going to be one, two, three stories organized around the theme.
On the podcast we're just putting out stories. So there's there's no more themes on the podcast which was a
conscious decision after a while because I don't feel like the world is organized into themes really.
And so we just started telling stories but each story does have to have
something a larger than itself. I mean that's sort of what you look for. You look for the sort of
Whitman-esque universe in a blade of grass kind of thing where there's some tiny thing that contains
everything and this was a case where you had a drama that was unfolding that was speaking to a
cataclysmic shift in the world of conservation and there what there used to be this idea that you save
you save everything and now there's this idea that like Triage is entered where you can only save
some things and you have to make hard decisions and one of the hard decisions was we have to embrace
the hunters and we have to have them as part of the conservation effort and that's become a primary
way to fund conservation efforts throughout Africa is by selling off endangered species and it felt
to us like a really big idea that was had impregnated this very tiny story and that's what you look for.
You look for tiny moments that contain things that are much, much bigger and sometimes it's only half
apparent and you just follow it hoping that that idea gets richer and more complicated and more you stare at
so imagine you're probably going down a lot of paths that don't end up leading anywhere.
Does that is that a strain on your time and your resources or do you have to scramble at the end to
pull something together or do you have enough stuff on the backburners that you can have an episode
ready to go when you need to. It's a lot of scrambleing. It's a lot of stuff. In some way like that
that how we began which was sort of in the dark not quite sure what we're doing is
fundamentally how we still are but you start to sort of like think of it like a poker player in a way.
You're measuring the sort of pot odds of every given bet that you're placing and you've got to have
like 40 bets happening at once because the math will say that six of those are going to win.
You know sometimes you get lucky and like 10 and sometimes you're screwed and you only two.
But over the sort of long term you place enough bets that you can pretty much be sure that
at every given two week chunk you've got something that's ready. But a lot of times you've got something that's
half ready and then you just have to like rescue it and sort of rob the future in order to put something out.
Close the loop yeah. You guys interview a ton of academics and scientists for the show and I'm

and they're choosing their words really carefully and that and all the stuff they said in the phone they're

going to try and understand what we do and and as we grew in audience what I would I began to notice
is that people came prepared to play whereas before you'd have to work really hard to get them to play
when the audience grew so large that people came understanding what the what the
was of the game were it was almost like they they they they knew you at that point and you didn't have to
explain yourself you didn't have to like because it would it would always be the case like in an interview you
spend the first hour just showing them what's what's permissible in a way so like you're interrupting them you're

can to make it not a formal thing and to let them know it's okay if you laugh it's okay if you tell

the part of the rules and so you spend a long time teaching people in the interview how to have the
conversation that you want to have as they teach you about their work right but over as we grew we didn't
have to do that anymore people just kind of came ready must have felt like a huge relief and a luxury in
some way it did but there's this sort of a curse to it which is that they come ready to make you an ambassador for
them when that's not your job you know and so we would do a lot of interviews where people would be
editing and composing the story they want you to tell in the moment when you is the journalist you
